Compared to geometry-based methods, which require complex indexing operations like lift in \cite{philion2020lift}, or look-up table in \cite{li2023fast}, our method is grounded in simplicity, leveraging only convolution and matrix multiplication. Our process begins by directly predicting the horizontal and vertical distributions of the Bird's Eye View (BEV) on 2D features. Following this, we obtain BEV features through a streamlined process of convolution and matrix multiplication. This approach not only simplifies the workflow but also enhances the efficiency and effectiveness of the spatial transformation in autonomous driving technologies.

Here's a polished version of the section:

In contrast to complex indexing operations used in geometry-based methods such as lift in \cite{philion2020lift} or look-up tables in \cite{li2023fast}, our method relies on simplicity while utilizing convolution and matrix multiplication. By directly predicting the horizontal and vertical distributions of the Bird's Eye View (BEV) on 2D features, we streamline the process of obtaining BEV features. This approach not only simplifies the workflow but also improves the efficiency and effectiveness of spatial transformation in autonomous driving technologies. Furthermore, our method enhances the simplicity and performance by avoiding the need for intricate indexing operations typically found in existing techniques.

Compared to geometry-based methods, which explicitly use projections between 2D and 3D spaces and therefore require complex indexing operations like lift in \cite{philion2020lift}, or look-up table in \cite{li2023fast}, our method directly predicts the horizontal and vertical BEV coordinates probability distributions corresponding to 2D features, and then combines these two to generate the overall BEV distribution. Afterwards, we combine the BEV distribution and 2D feature to obtain the resulting BEV features. Since we only use convolution and matrix multiplication, our methods has a higher compatibility to on-vehicle chips.

Here's a polished version of the section:

In comparison to geometry-based methods that rely on explicit projections between 2D and 3D spaces and subsequently entail complex indexing operations such as lift in \cite{philion2020lift} or look-up tables in \cite{li2023fast}, our approach takes a direct route. Instead of performing intricate indexing operations, our method directly predicts the probability distributions of horizontal and vertical Bird's Eye View (BEV) coordinates based on 2D features. We then combine these distributions to generate an overall BEV distribution. By combining the BEV distribution with the 2D feature, we obtain the resulting BEV features using only convolution and matrix multiplication. This design choice not only simplifies the process but also enhances compatibility with on-vehicle chips, making our method more efficient and suitable for autonomous driving technologies.

Our framework integrates four components for efficiency: an image backbone that extracts 2D features from multi-view images; a view transformer, which uses camera parameters via convolutions solely to predict horizontal and vertical BEV distributions for 2D feature maps; a learnable PCA module that projects the high-dimensional matrices to lower-dimensional principal component space and implement the matrix multiplication to obtain the BEV feature maps; and finally, a 3D detection head is applied to generate 3D bounding boxes.

Our framework comprises four key components that work together to optimize efficiency. Firstly, we utilize an image backbone to extract 2D features from multi-view images. Secondly, we incorporate a view transformer that solely employs convolutions and utilizes camera parameters to predict horizontal and vertical Bird's Eye View (BEV) distributions for the 2D feature maps. 

To further enhance efficiency, we introduce a learnable PCA module that projects high-dimensional matrices to a lower-dimensional principal component space. This module then performs matrix multiplication operations to obtain the BEV feature maps. 

Lastly, we apply a 3D detection head that utilizes the generated BEV feature maps to produce accurate 3D bounding boxes. By integrating these four components, our framework optimizes efficiency while maintaining high performance levels.

Our framework comprises four components: firstly an image backbone extracts 2D features from multi-view images. Secondly, a view transformer directly predicts horizontal and vertical BEV distributions for 2D feature maps using camera parameters via convolutions solely. To further improve efficiency, a learnable PCA module that projects the high-dimensional matrices to lower-dimensional principal component space and implement the matrix multiplication to obtain the BEV feature maps. Lastly, a 3D detection head is applied to generate 3D bounding boxes.

Our framework incorporates four key components. First, an image backbone is used to extract 2D features from multi-view images. Secondly, a view transformer employs convolutions and directly predicts the horizontal and vertical Bird's Eye View (BEV) distributions for the 2D feature maps using camera parameters. 

To enhance efficiency, we introduce a learnable PCA module that projects the high-dimensional matrices to a lower-dimensional principal component space and implements matrix multiplication operations to obtain the BEV feature maps. 

Finally, a 3D detection head is applied to generate accurate 3D bounding boxes using the BEV feature maps. By integrating these four components, our framework achieves efficient and effective results in 3D object detection.

Generally, classification is commonly used to predict categorical distributions and promote sharp distributions to select the correct class. Instead of discrete BEV coordinates, we predict the coordinate distributions since we need to consider the estimation uncertainty. Therefore, the regression task turns into the classification task and correspondingly, the cross entropy loss is applied to supervise the BEV horizontal and vertical distribution prediction.

In our approach, we deviate from the traditional regression task and adopt a classification approach to predict coordinate distributions instead of discrete BEV coordinates. This is necessary to account for the estimation uncertainty involved in the task. By treating the regression task as a classification task, we can leverage techniques like cross entropy loss to effectively supervise and train the prediction of horizontal and vertical BEV distributions. This enables us to handle the uncertainty in a more robust manner, ensuring accurate and reliable results for our framework.

In our vanilla FC-BEV, spatial transformation requires a large amount of memory footprint and calculations. As shown in Eq. \ref{eq:bev_distribution} to generate $M_{2d \to bev}$,  $H_{bev} \times W_{bev} \times W_{2d} \times H_{2d}$ calculations and memory footprint are required. As the spatial size of 2D feature maps increases, the time and storage consumption rise quadratically, which is also the case in Eq. \ref{eq:bev_features}, where $H_{bev} \times W_{bev} \times W_{2d} \times H_{2d} \times C$ calculations are required. Moreover, due to the sparse mapping between 2D and BEV spaces, the horizontal and vertical BEV distributions $P_{2d}^{x_{bev}}$ and $P_{2d}^{y_{bev}}$ are also sparse with large compressible rooms. To address these characteristics, we proposes a learnable-PCA module, which consists of only convolution and matrix multiplication, designed to utilize the principle components in the intermediate matrices and enhance the efficiency of our framework.

In our vanilla FC-BEV model, spatial transformation involves substantial memory utilization and computational requirements. As shown in Eq. (1) to generate $M_{2d \to bev}$, it necessitates $H_{bev} \times W_{bev} \times W_{2d} \times H_{2d}$ computations and memory footprint. As the spatial size of 2D feature maps increases, the time and storage consumption escalates quadratically, as also evident in Eq. (2), where $H_{bev} \times W_{bev} \times W_{2d} \times H_{2d} \times C$ computations are necessary. 

Additionally, due to the sparsity in the mapping between 2D and BEV spaces, the horizontal and vertical BEV distributions $P_{2d}^{x_{bev}}$ and $P_{2d}^{y_{bev}}$ also exhibit sparsity with considerable compressible potential. To address these specific characteristics, we propose a learnable-PCA module that solely comprises convolution and matrix multiplication operations. This module is designed to leverage the principal components in the intermediate matrices, thereby improving the efficiency of our framework while effectively addressing the sparse nature of the BEV distributions.

As the demand for accurate 3D perception grows, researchers have explored various methods to the model performance. Some of them utilize LiDAR data as input \cite{zhou2018voxelnet, lang2019pointpillars, yin2021center, qi2017pointnet, qi2017pointnet++}. However, LiDAR's performance is hindered in bad weather conditions, and its cost is prohibitive for mass production. Monocular methods using only camera data, such as \cite{liu2020smoke, cai2020monocular, wu2023monopgc, wu2023attention}, offer a more practical solution. Combining single-view outputs from monocular models into the Bird's Eye View (BEV) perspective centered at the ego vehicle has been attempted but with challenges: notable time consumption, poor accuracy especially for intersection areas between different views, requiring Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS), and other limitations.

To improve the performance of 3D perception models, researchers have explored various methods. Some of these methods utilize LiDAR data as input, as seen in works such as \cite{zhou2018voxelnet, lang2019pointpillars, yin2021center, qi2017pointnet, qi2017pointnet++}. However, LiDAR systems face limitations in adverse weather conditions and can be cost-prohibitive for mass production. 

Alternatively, monocular methods that solely rely on camera data, such as \cite{liu2020smoke, cai2020monocular, wu2023monopgc, wu2023attention}, offer a more practical solution. These methods use only a single camera view to generate outputs. However, integrating these single-view outputs into a Bird's Eye View (BEV) perspective centered at the ego vehicle poses challenges.

These challenges include notable time consumption, poor accuracy, especially in the intersection areas between different views, the requirement for Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS), and other limitations. Overcoming these challenges is crucial to ensure accurate and efficient 3D perception in complex scenarios.
shorter and more academic

To improve 3D perception models, researchers have explored different approaches. Some methods utilize LiDAR data as input, yet its limitations in adverse weather conditions and high cost hinder widespread use. Monocular methods using only camera data provide a more practical solution. However, integrating single-view outputs into a Bird's Eye View (BEV) perspective centered at the ego vehicle presents challenges such as time consumption, accuracy issues in intersection areas, and the need for Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS). Addressing these challenges is crucial for accurate and efficient 3D perception.

To address the aforementioned challenges, an end-to-end approach based on BEV perspective has been proposed to solve vision tasks including lane detection \cite{wang2023bev, chen2022persformer}, vectorized map generation \cite{liao2022maptr, liu2023vectormapnet, liao2023maptrv2}, 3D semantic segmentation \cite{peng2022bevsegformer, xu2022cobevt, ng2020bevseg}, and 3D object detection \cite{zhu2020deformable, wang2022detr3d, liu2022petr, wu2022surround}. These approaches consistently incorporate different BEV spatial transformation modules, which determine the overall model performance.

To overcome these challenges, researchers have proposed an end-to-end approach based on the Bird's Eye View (BEV) perspective. This approach has been successfully applied to various vision tasks, including lane detection \cite{wang2023bev, chen2022persformer}, vectorized map generation \cite{liao2022maptr, liu2023vectormapnet, liao2023maptrv2}, 3D semantic segmentation \cite{peng2022bevsegformer, xu2022cobevt, ng2020bevseg}, and 3D object detection \cite{zhu2020deformable, wang2022detr3d, liu2022petr, wu2022surround}. 

A common characteristic among these approaches is the incorporation of various BEV spatial transformation modules, which play a crucial role in determining the overall performance of the models. By leveraging the BEV perspective, these methods achieve more accurate and efficient results in a range of computer vision tasks.

Consequently, a growing volume of research has focused on generating 3D spatial BEV representations from 2D images from single or multi-view cameras. The conventional Inverse Perspective Mapping (IPM) methods\cite{bertozz1998stereo, zhang2014robust, jeong2016adaptive} calculate the homography matrix derived from 2D images through the camera calibrations. It stitches together multi-view images to create a 3D BEV representation. However, this approach heavily depends on fixed calibration parameters, flat groun hypothesis and is susceptible to limited robustness. Especially, it is very unfriendly to moving or tall objects.

As a result, there has been a surge in research focusing on generating 3D spatial Bird's Eye View (BEV) representations from 2D images captured by single or multi-view cameras. Traditional methods, such as Inverse Perspective Mapping (IPM) techniques \cite{bertozz1998stereo, zhang2014robust, jeong2016adaptive}, employ homography matrices derived from camera calibrations to stitch together multi-view images and create a 3D BEV representation. However, these methods heavily rely on fixed calibration parameters and assume a flat ground hypothesis, making them less robust. They particularly struggle with moving or tall objects, leading to limited effectiveness in those scenarios.
